title: The US Women's Jury Movements and Strategic Adaptation: A More Just Verdict By Holly J. McCammon Cambridge University Press. 2012. 298 pages. $99.00 cloth, $29.99 paper
abstract: Upon ratification of the Nineteenth Amendment to the US Constitution granting women the right to vote, a few states assumed that women's citizenship rights would automatically be extended to the jury box. But the majority of states continued to deny women access to this fundamental right. Thus, even after winning the decades-long battle for suffrage, women in over thirty states still lacked the right to a jury of their peers in criminal and civil courts. State by state, women mobilized to secure jury rights. In some states (e.g., Wisconsin, California, Vermont), the pace of reform was swift. In other states (e.g., Alabama, South Carolina, Mississippi), women did not win the right to serve on juries until the late 1960s. Why and how did the movements in some states secure jury rights so quickly while others took several decades? Why were some jury campaigns strategic in their approaches from the onset while others were not strategic until much later in their struggles? These are the primary questions Holly McCammon seeks to answer in The US Women's Jury Movements and Strategic Adaptation.
